title: Presence of Antimicrobial Resistance Genes in Organomineral Fertilizer
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"c7gq3","text":"This protocol details the steps for detecting various antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs), including tetA, tetX, tetO, ermB, sul1, blaCTX-M-32, and intI1, using PCR and gel electrophoresis. Starting with DNA extraction from organomineral fertilizer samples, the method describes PCR reaction preparation with specific primers and controls, followed by visualization of results on an agarose gel. Ethidium bromide and SYBR Safe are used for staining, with appropriate safety precautions. This technique allows for the identification of target resistance genes, indicated by the presence of corresponding DNA bands under UV analysis.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":100,"length":27},{"style":"italic","offset":147,"length":4},{"style":"sub","offset":133,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a0qjr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6k88c","text":"In addition, the protocol is specifically designed to assess ARGs in organomineral fertilizers (OMF), which are innovative agricultural products that integrate organic matter, such as animal manure or plant waste, with mineral fertilizers. This sustainable approach enhances soil fertility and crop productivity while promoting effective by-product utilization and animal waste management. However, the manure component in OMF can serve as a reservoir for ARGs, raising concerns about the spread of antibiotic resistance in agricultural environments. By identifying specific ARGs in OMF, this protocol offers critical insights into the environmental implications of these fertilizers and supports the development of strategies to mitigate the risks associated with antibiotic resistance.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"biubb","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"3vsv6","text":"Appendix B –  Result Interpretation","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":13},{"style":"bold","offset":14,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ai73m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"53t1f","text":"•\tPositive results will show a DNA band corresponding to the target gene.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6i3te","text":"•\tNegative results will show no bands, indicating the absence of resistance genes.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"74e8i","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6tlrq","text":"•\tFigure 13. Example of agarose gel electrophoresis. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":1,"length":52}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ca62j","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ec63f","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7iomj","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"e5bnc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4n6sd","text":"Appendix C – Positive Control (gBlock) Sequences","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":48}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2cvo5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fa0v5","text":"The gBlocks Gene Fragments, produced by Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT), are double-stranded synthetic DNA fragments that are sequence-verified and ready for use in molecular biology workflows. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fdfjr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gi8l","text":"Structure and Features","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":22}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"12ejg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aefkc","text":"•\tSequence Orientation: The gBlock sequence is written in the 5’ to 3’ orientation, as is standard for representing DNA sequences.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":2,"length":21},{"style":"bold","offset":62,"length":20}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dg8ak","text":"•\tTarget and Flanking Sequences: Each gBlock contains the desired target sequence flanked by extra sequences on either end. These additional sequences may include buffer regions, restriction sites, or regions necessary for experimental designs like cloning or PCR.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":2,"length":31},{"style":"bold","offset":65,"length":16},{"style":"bold","offset":93,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9hshd","text":"•\tPurpose of this gBlock: This specific gBlock serves as a control for multiple targets. steps: QIAGEN® DNeasy® PowerSoil® Pro Figure 1. DNeasy PowerSoil Pro Kit.
Begin by measuring 250 mg of organomineral fertilizer. Figure 2. Organomineral fertilizer formulations.
Then, add the measured organomineral fertilizer and 800 µL of Solution CD1 to the PowerBead Pro tubes . Figure 3. DNeasy PowerSoil Pro Kit Solutions.
Vortex the sample in the Bead Beater for 00:00:45 at max speed.
Ensuring tubes are balanced, secured, and the bead beater’s lid is closed for safety. Figure 4. Bead Beater used to Vortex the sample.
Centrifuge tubes at 15000 x g, °C, 1 minute .
Ensuring tubes are balanced symmetrically in the rotor to prevent damage. Use balancing weights or dummy tubes if processing fewer samples. Figure 5. Example of tubes in Centrifuge.
Transfer 500 µL - 600 µL of supernatant to a new 2 mL Microcentrifuge Tube.
Add 200 µL Solution CD2 and vortex for 00:00:05 .
Centrifuge at 15000 x g, °C, 1 minute . Transfer 700 µL supernatant to a new tube.
Add 600 µL Solution CD3 , vortex for 00:00:05 .
Repeat go to step #11 for remaining lysate.
Add 500 µL Solution EA , centrifuge at 15000 x g, °C, 1 minute , and discard flow-through.
Add 500 µL Solution C5 , centrifuge at 15000 x g, °C, 1 minute , discard flow-through, and centrifuge again for 00:02:00 .
Elute DNA by adding 100 µL Solution C6 to the filter and centrifuging at 15000 x g, °C, 1 minute .
Store DNA at -30 °C . Figure 6. The DNA is stored in the freezer at -30 °C and labeled with a unique recognizable identifier.

Prepare PCR reactions in 0.2 mL strip tubes: Figure 7. Materials used to evaluate the presence of antibiotic resistance genes in organomineral fertilizers. oMaster Mix JumpStart Red Taq (P0982-800rxn): 12.5 µL oPCR Water: 5.5 µL oForward Primer (10 µM): 1 µL oReverse Primer (10 µM): 1 µL oDNA Sample: 5 µL (Negative control: PCR Water in place of DNA sample) (Positive control: gBlock variety in place of DNA sample)

Add gBlock variety listed in Appendix A (Guidelines & Warnings Section), for Tet and antibiotic-resistant genes as follows: o tetA : 1 µL gBlock 12 + 4 µL PCR water o tetX : 2 µL gBlock 10 + 3 µL PCR water o tetO (gBlock 13), ermB ( gBlock 17), intI1 ( gBlock 19), bla CTX-M-32 (gBlock 18), sul 1 (gBlock 3): 3 µL gBlock + 2 µL PCR water

Gently mix the contents of the tubes by stirring, then place the reaction tubes in the thermocycler, following specific settings for Tet and antibiotic-resistant genes listed in Table 1 and Table 2 . Figure 8. 0.2 mL strip tubes with PCR reaction being placed in the thermocycler. %s %s %s %s
Figure 9. Gel casting tray, and gel comb. Prepare the agarose gel by dissolving 0.4 g of agarose and 50 mL of 1x TAE buffer in an Erlenmeyer Flask and mix gently to combine.
Cover the opening of the Erlenmeyer flask with a paper towel.
Place the mixture in the microwave for 00:00:30 , until the solution begins to bubble.
Check for clarity, and if necessary, repeat until the solution is clear.
Be sure to use thermal gloves when handling the bottle to prevent burns.
Add 2 µL ethidium bromide (for tetA, tetX, tetO ), or 4 µL SYBR safe (for ermB , intI1 , bla CTX-M-32 , sul 1 ).
After heating, carefully pour the mixture from the bottle into the gel casting tray, ensuring the gel comb is already in place.
Allow the mixture to cool to Room temperature until it solidifies and develops a gelatinous texture. Figure 10. SYBR safe containers.
Place the gel, along with the casting tray, into the Gel Electrophoresis Apparatus.
Add 0.25x TAE buffer to the tank, ensuring the gel is fully submerged and completely covered by the buffer.
Load 10 µL of the PCR amplicon into the gel wells ( 40 µl ).
Run gel at 130 V for 00:30:00 .

Analyze gel under UV light to detect DNA bands for positive results. CAUTION : UV light can damage your eyes. Eye protection is required. Figure 11. UV light analyzer and protective glasses used to analyze the gel.
Transfer 650 µL lysate to MB Spin Column. Centrifuge at 15000 x g, °C, 1 minute . Discard flow-through.
Turn on the scale, place the tube on it, and tare to zero before carefully weighing the sample.
Refer to the safety information for using ethidium bromide provided in Appendix A (In 'Warnings' section) for detailed guidance.
See Appendix B (in 'Guidelines' section) for the results interpretations.
creator: Lisa M. Durso
